title: practical english usage - Part 145
content:
live /laiv/ (meaning 'not dead’ ) Compare: My elder sister is a pilot. She's three years older than me.

elder and eldest Compare: must be used. Common examples: Some adjectives are used only (or mostly) before nouns. After verbs, other words 2 adjectives        used    only    before     nouns That dress is new, isn't it? She looks rich.  I feel unhappy.

'predicative position'.

b  after be, seem, look, become and other linking verbs (►   11). This is called For adjectives before personal pronouns (e.g. Poor you!), ►     174.7.

ɪn modern English, this only happens in a few cases.

He came from his palace grand.

especially in poetry and songs.

In older English (►  318.10), it was quite common to put adjectives after nouns, He's going out with a rich businesswoman.

The new secretary doesn't like me.

a  with a noun, usually before it. This is called 'attributive position'.

Most adjectives can go in two main places in a sentence.

Another possible position for adjectives is after the object, in the structure 4 verb    + object     + adjective a healthy/fit man    Nurses take care of sick people.

- He's very well.    - You look ill.

grammar *185  adjectives with and My soul is exotic, mysterious, incomprehensible.

In a very literary style, and is sometimes left out.

You're like a winter's day: short, dark and dirty.

He was tall, dark and handsome.
